name: Ascend Firing Pin
tagline: Hardware-aware job validation for Huawei Ascend AI clusters
description: A specialized MLOps tool for managing training jobs on Huawei Ascend NPU hardware. It validates training configurations against the specific memory hierarchy and interconnect topology of the Ascend architecture to prevent catastrophic job failures before they consume expensive compute.
design_writeup: Unlike standard CUDA monitors, the Firing Pin is deeply integrated with the Ascend hardware stack (MindSpore/CANN). It functions as a pre-flight checklist that simulates the memory allocation and communication patterns of a job across the NPU mesh. It specifically monitors for 'hot spots' in the interconnect and VMEM overflows that are common in non-SIMT architectures. The system would provide a dashboard for cluster health and a CLI for developers to dry-run their code. The MVP would focus on job scheduling optimization, ensuring that large-scale training jobs are only launched when the physical topology of available NPUs minimizes latency.

tagline: Custom storage controller silicon for hyperscale efficiency
description: A specialized storage controller and firmware stack designed for Azure's fleet. It offloads RAID, compression, and IO management from the CPU to dedicated silicon to expand cloud capacity.
design_writeup: This project aims to replicate the AWS Nitro success specifically for storage. The controller is designed to sit between the NVMe drives and the host CPU, communicating via PCIe. It uses an FPGA-based prototype that handles NVMe-over-Fabrics (NVMe-oF) and hardware-accelerated compression/encryption. The software layer is a custom hypervisor module that treats the offload engine as a transparent storage tier, freeing up to 20% of host CPU cycles previously spent on IO wait and management. The architecture ensures low-latency RDMA access for remote storage volumes. The MVP involves building the firmware for a standard PCIe controller and demonstrating a reduction in CPU overhead for common database workloads on Azure VM instances.

name: Branch-Predictive KV Manager
tagline: CPU-style branch prediction for KV cache eviction
description: A high-performance memory management system that treats KV cache eviction as a high-dimensional branch prediction problem. It optimizes memory bandwidth by predicting which tokens will be needed for future turns in long conversations.
design_writeup: This system applies classical computer architecture principles to deep learning memory management. It treats the KV cache as a multi-tier hierarchy (SRAM/HBM/DRAM) and uses predictive heuristics—such as cumulative attention scores, recency, and structural position—to determine which tensors to evict. By viewing attention as a form of 'data branch,' the manager can pre-fetch necessary KV pairs into fast memory before they are requested by the model. The architecture involves a C++ backend integrated with vLLM or Triton. The main challenge is balancing the compute cost of the predictor against the memory bandwidth saved. The MVP would be an optimized KV allocator that reduces 'time to first token' in multi-turn chat applications.

design_writeup: While SONiC has dominated the data center, it lacks the protocol complexity required for campus deployments (e.g., 802.1X, PoE management, and RADIUS). The architecture follows a microservices model where each networking protocol runs in its own container, managed by a centralized orchestration engine. The stack would be written in C++ and Python, leveraging the existing SONiC codebase while adding a new 'Campus-CLI' and a web-based management GUI to lower the barrier for traditional IT admins. The MVP would focus on basic PoE and 802.1X support on a limited set of whitebox hardware.

name: Cloud VFX Renderer
tagline: Next-gen ray tracing optimized for enterprise AI GPU clusters.
description: A high-performance rendering engine designed to repurpose underutilized AI compute clusters for VFX production. It allows cloud providers to pivot massive GPU capacity from LLM training to embarrassingly parallel rendering tasks.
design_writeup: The Cloud VFX Renderer leverages NVIDIA's OptiX and Vulkan Ray Tracing frameworks to run high-fidelity rendering tasks on H100/A100 clusters. The system uses a distributed scheduler that breaks down frame sequences into independent tasks dispatched across thousands of nodes. It utilizes the same InfiniBand interconnects found in AI clusters to facilitate rapid streaming of large assets like textures and geometry caches. The engine is fully compatible with the Universal Scene Description (USD) format, allowing it to integrate into major studio pipelines. The stack includes C++ for core kernels and Python for orchestration. A major challenge is ensuring bit-perfect output parity with traditional CPU renderers. The MVP will provide a Blender and Houdini plugin that enables one-click rendering to cloud GPU infrastructure, significantly reducing render times for complex scenes.

name: ColabKube Bridge
tagline: Connect Google Colab directly to your private Kubernetes cluster resources.
description: A networking and orchestration toolkit that allows Google Colab to offload heavy machine learning workloads to a user's private Kubernetes cluster. This enables data scientists to use Colab's familiar UI while leveraging their own high-performance GPUs and enterprise-grade hardware.
design_writeup:
ColabKube Bridge aims to bypass the hardware limitations of Google Colab's free and pro tiers by using Colab as a frontend for private infrastructure. The system consists of two primary components: a local Python client library and a server-side 'Kube-Agent' deployed within a Kubernetes cluster. The Kube-Agent is a modified Jupyter kernel gateway that sits behind a secure tunnel (using WireGuard or Tailscale) to accept incoming connections from the public internet without exposing the cluster.

When a user initiates a session, the Colab-side library establishes a secure gRPC link to the Kube-Agent. The library intercepts Python cell execution and redirects the payload to the K8s cluster, where the Kube-Agent schedules a pod with the requested resources (CPU, RAM, or specific GPUs like NVIDIA A100s). The results and logs are then streamed back to the Colab interface in real-time. This requires sophisticated state management to ensure that the local Python environment in the K8s pod matches the expectations of the Colab notebook. 

Technical challenges include managing the lifecycle of ephemeral pods, handling data persistence between sessions, and ensuring secure authentication. The MVP will include a CLI tool for cluster administrators to deploy the Agent via Helm and a simple Python package for users. The first version will support basic 'Remote Kernel' functionality, allowing users to toggle between Google's default runtime and their private K8s cluster with a single command, effectively turning Colab into a thin client for enterprise-grade compute.
claude_prompt:
Build ColabKube Bridge: A networking and orchestration toolkit that allows Google Colab to offload heavy machine learning workloads to a user's private Kubernetes cluster. This enables data scientists to use Colab's familiar UI while leveraging their own high-performance GPUs and enterprise-grade hardware.

name: CollectiveOps Debugger
tagline: High-fidelity visual debugger for NCCL and collective operations
description: A specialized infrastructure tool for debugging synchronization hangs and performance bottlenecks in large-scale GPU clusters. It visualizes NCCL collective operations (All-Reduce, Broadcast) across thousands of nodes to identify straggler GPUs and network topology issues.
design_writeup: CollectiveOps Debugger is a mission-critical tool designed for hyperscale AI labs and cloud providers. It works by instrumenting the NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communications Library) layer to capture high-resolution timing data for every collective operation initiated across a cluster. The core architecture involves a distributed agent running on each compute node that pipes telemetry to a centralized time-series database. A sophisticated frontend visualizes the 'sync-gradient' across the cluster, using heatmaps and Gantt charts to highlight specific GPUs that are causing global training stalls (stragglers). Unlike generic profilers, this tool is optimized for the 'Valley of Death' scale (1,000+ GPUs), providing topological awareness to see if hangs are related to specific switches or racks. The tech stack includes C++, NVML for hardware health monitoring, and a high-throughput data pipeline using gRPC. The business value is extreme: for a large training run, reducing sync overhead by even 5% can save millions of dollars in compute time. The MVP would target internal infrastructure teams at major AI labs with a self-hosted deployment model.
source_conversations: Harvard vs UCSD for ML systems

name: DIME
tagline: Runtime monitoring for distributed GPU training health
description: A runtime monitoring tool that detects hardware bit flips and silent communication corruption during massive AI training runs. It specializes in monitoring NCCL health and identifying silent failures in heterogeneous clusters.
design_writeup: DIME (Distributed Infrastructure Memory Examiner) is a runtime monitoring system specifically engineered for the unique stability requirements of distributed AI training. Unlike general-purpose cloud monitoring tools, DIME focuses on the complex interplay between GPU hardware health and the collective communication performance of a cluster. The system operates as a non-intrusive distributed daemon that hooks into the NVIDIA Collective Communications Library (NCCL) and the CUDA Profiling Tools Interface (CUPTI). It is designed to monitor for "silent" hardware failures such as multi-bit flips in HBM that might bypass standard ECC memory corrections, or network packet corruption that causes collective calls (like AllReduce) to hang indefinitely. The tech stack utilizes eBPF for low-overhead network monitoring at the kernel level and Prometheus for time-series data storage and alerting. The core architectural challenge is minimizing the "observer effect"; the monitoring must be lightweight enough to avoid stealing compute cycles or impacting the high-speed interconnect bandwidth. DIME solves this by using asynchronous sampling techniques and efficient ring buffers for telemetry data. The MVP will provide a real-time dashboard and alerting system for NCCL timeouts, version-specific deadlocks, and throughput degradation. This allows infrastructure engineers to identify and isolate failing nodes, checkpoint the training job, and restart it before significant compute resources are wasted on a corrupted or stalled run.
claude_prompt:

description: A CAD platform for data center design that treats building layout as a graph embedding problem. It integrates architectural constraints, civil engineering, and hardware requirements like NVLink cable lengths to find the globally optimal placement of switches and racks.
design_writeup: The core engine uses graph embedding algorithms to map nodes (GPUs and Racks) to physical 3D space. It utilizes a constraint satisfaction solver to handle power delivery, thermal envelopes, and strict cable length bounds. The software is designed for systems engineers, with a configuration-file-driven UX that automates the placement of every switch in a large-scale campus.

name: Dataflow Interconnect Fabric
tagline: Networking as an extension of the chip itself.
description: A programmable interconnect startup focusing on chiplet-to-chiplet communication that treats networking fabric as integrated silicon logic.
design_writeup: The Dataflow Interconnect Fabric is a project aimed at dissolving the boundary between networking and compute in high-performance data centers. Moving beyond traditional SerDes and NIC designs, the system integrates the networking logic directly into the chiplet packaging (2.5D/3D). The core functionality is a programmable switching layer that allows for the fluid movement of data across heterogeneous accelerators (CPUs, GPUs, and custom XPUs) with near-zero latency. The technical stack involves SystemVerilog for ASIC design, the P4 language for defining network behavior, and a custom SDN (Software-Defined Networking) controller. The architecture is designed to compete with proprietary interconnects like NVLink by offering an open, high-bandwidth alternative for custom silicon providers (e.g., Google or Meta). The MVP scope is a functional RTL simulation of a multi-chiplet environment demonstrating significantly reduced hops and latency compared to PCIe-based systems. The main challenge is the enormous capital and R&D requirements of semiconductor development and the necessity of achieving industry-leading SerDes performance to compete with Broadcom.
source_conversations: Projects discussed over the past month

name: DynamoDB Magnitude
tagline: Native vector search integrated into the DynamoDB ecosystem.
description: A proposed extension or managed service for AWS that adds native vector database capabilities to DynamoDB. It provides high-scale embedding storage and similarity search for AI-driven applications.
design_writeup: DynamoDB Magnitude is envisioned as a purpose-built vector engine that lives within the AWS DynamoDB infrastructure. Unlike existing solutions that require a separate database instance, Magnitude would allow users to store high-dimensional embeddings as a specialized attribute type within standard Dynamo tables. The system would implement an HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) index or a similar approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) algorithm natively within the storage layer. This ensures that vector searches benefit from the same IAM security, VPC isolation, and global replication that DynamoDB users expect. Integration with AWS Bedrock and SageMaker would be seamless, allowing developers to generate embeddings via NIM or SageMaker endpoints and store them directly. The design addresses the massive gap in the AWS stack where teams currently have to manage third-party vector DBs or use general-purpose RDS extensions. The scope includes a managed API for performing K-nearest neighbor searches with sub-10ms latency at massive scale.

name: EdgeCast Transcoding Protocol
tagline: Distributed edge video transcoding on user hardware
description: A protocol that allows video platforms like Snapchat or TikTok to offload compute-intensive video transcoding (AV1/VP9) to the uploader's high-performance mobile hardware. It utilizes idle GPU/NPU cycles on modern smartphones to reduce server-side infrastructure costs.
design_writeup: EdgeCast is a client-side SDK that detects hardware-accelerated encoding capabilities on mobile devices (e.g., Apple M3/M4, Snapdragon 8 Gen 3). When a user records or selects a video for upload, the SDK performs a multi-pass encode locally into the most efficient codec supported (AV1 or VP9) before transmission. This shifts the 'compute tax' from the data center to the device. The architecture includes a negotiation layer that queries the server for the desired target bitrates and resolutions, then utilizes WebAssembly or native hardware wrappers to execute the encode. The technical challenge is balancing battery consumption and thermals; the SDK would need to implement a 'thermal throttling' mechanism and potentially only run during peak charge states or as a background task. The MVP scope would be a React Native or Flutter plugin that demonstrates a 40% reduction in file size for uploads compared to standard H.264, verified by a cloud-side metadata check.

Build EdgeCast Transcoding Protocol: A protocol that allows video platforms like Snapchat or TikTok to offload compute-intensive video transcoding (AV1/VP9) to the uploader's high-performance mobile hardware. It utilizes idle GPU/NPU cycles on modern smartphones to reduce server-side infrastructure costs.

name: EdgeTorch Abstraction Layer
tagline: Portable PyTorch for Department of Defense edge computing.
description: A compiler and runtime abstraction layer that allows PyTorch models to run seamlessly on heterogeneous edge hardware (Qualcomm, ARM, custom FPGA) without vendor-specific CUDA-like lock-in. Targeted at secure, decentralized military and tactical hardware.
design_writeup: EdgeTorch focuses on breaking the CUDA dependency by targeting the MLIR (Multi-Level Intermediate Representation) ecosystem. It functions as a custom backend for PyTorch 2.0's 'torch.compile' stack, lowering high-level tensors into optimized kernels for non-NVIDIA silicon. The architecture utilizes a hardware-agnostic intermediate layer that can be targeted by vendors to provide drivers for specialized edge chips used in drones and satellites. The technical challenge lies in kernel optimization—achieving performance parity with hand-tuned CUDA without the 15-year head start. The MVP scope involves a specialized containerized runtime for ARM-based tactical devices that can execute quantized PyTorch models with minimal latency. It would leverage TVM or OpenXLA for low-level code generation. This project positions itself as an open-source standard for 'sovereign' hardware, making it attractive for government contracts where vendor lock-in is a national security risk.
source_conversations: PyTorch portability and NVIDIA's market dominance

name: Enterprise Nexus
tagline: The unified middleware for modern cloud ERP integration
description: A cloud-native integration platform that replaces legacy middleware to connect major enterprise SaaS applications like Salesforce, ServiceNow, and Workday into a single cohesive dashboard. It provides a standardized data bus for cross-platform automation and unified visibility.
design_writeup: Enterprise Nexus is designed to be the definitive 'Cloud Operating System' for the modern enterprise, directly addressing the fragmentation caused by the proliferation of specialized SaaS platforms. The core functionality revolves around a unified middleware layer that abstracts the APIs of major enterprise applications—Salesforce, Workday, ServiceNow, and others—into a single, standardized data model. The tech stack will leverage a high-performance backend written in Go, utilizing a microservices architecture to ensure scalability and fault tolerance. For real-time data synchronization, the platform will implement an event-driven architecture using Apache Kafka as the primary message broker. This allows the system to capture state changes in one application and propagate them across the entire ecosystem in milliseconds. The architecture includes a sophisticated 'Adapter Framework' that handles the nuances of each third-party API, including OAuth2 credential management, rate-limiting, and schema mapping. A central GraphQL API serves as the primary interface for internal developers, enabling them to execute complex cross-platform queries that would otherwise require multiple API calls and manual data joining. The primary technical challenge lies in managing data consistency across systems with differing transaction models; the platform will use a 'Saga pattern' to manage distributed transactions and ensure eventual consistency. Security is also paramount, and the platform will feature end-to-end encryption for data in transit and at rest, integrated with enterprise SSO providers. The MVP scope will focus on a 'Sales-to-Service' pipeline, synchronizing account data between Salesforce and ServiceNow, and providing a unified dashboard that visualizes the status of a customer across both platforms. This provides a clean, modern alternative to legacy middleware solutions like Oracle’s, which are often difficult to maintain in a cloud-first world.

name: Ephemeral Agent Identity Manager
tagline: Secure task-bound credentialing for autonomous agents
description: A workload identity framework for AI agents that issues short-lived, task-specific credentials. Instead of long-lived API keys, agents receive tokens that expire immediately upon task completion, mitigating the risk of credential theft.
design_writeup: The Ephemeral Agent Identity Manager is an infrastructure-level security tool designed to solve the secret sprawl problem in autonomous agentic systems. Traditional agents often rely on long-lived API keys which are vulnerable to exfiltration. This tool replaces static secrets with a dynamic, short-lived credentialing system based on the SPIFFE/SPIRE standard. The architecture involves a control plane that issues X.509 certificates or short-lived JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) to agent processes based on cryptographically verified attestation—checking the agent's unique process ID, the host environment, and its checksum. These credentials are task-bound, meaning they are minted only when an agent begins a specific workflow and are automatically revoked or expired upon completion. A technical challenge is integrating this system with existing cloud IAM roles, requiring the manager to act as an OIDC provider that maps ephemeral agent identities to granular cloud permissions. The MVP scope consists of a central identity server, a sidecar agent for credential rotation, and a set of SDKs for popular agent frameworks like LangChain or AutoGPT to allow them to fetch and use these credentials seamlessly without manual key management.
claude_prompt:
Build Ephemeral Agent Identity Manager: A workload identity framework for AI agents that issues short-lived, task-specific credentials. Instead of long-lived API keys, agents receive tokens that expire immediately upon task completion, mitigating the risk of credential theft.

name: Expert Affinity MoE Scheduler
tagline: Route queries to pre-loaded Mixture-of-Expert weights for 10x inference speed
description: A serving-layer scheduler that treats expert affinity as a first-class routing dimension. It predicts which experts (MoE) are needed for a specific query and routes it to a GPU that already has those weights resident in VRAM, eliminating the need to load the entire 1T+ parameter model.
design_writeup: The system extends existing inference engines like vLLM to include a speculative prefetching and routing layer. It partitions the full expert pool of a massive MoE model (e.g., Kimi k2.6) across a cluster of GPUs. By analyzing the query embedding before the full forward pass, the router predicts the top-k expert activation pattern with ~98% accuracy and dispatches the request to the specific 'B200' node holding those experts. This avoids the latency of swapping weights from system RAM to VRAM and enables serving massive models on a fraction of the hardware otherwise required. Key challenges include maintaining the prediction accuracy of the router and managing the communication overhead between partitions.
source_conversations: Kimi k2.6 pricing and open source status
claude_prompt:

name: Firing Pin
tagline: Pre-flight observability and diagnostic warnings for GPU clusters.
description: A diagnostic tool for ML engineers to validate GPU cluster health and job configuration before execution. It catches common failure modes like NCCL topology issues, library mismatches, or configuration errors to minimize wasted compute costs.
design_writeup: This tool provides a critical pre-flight check for high-performance ML workloads, focusing on cluster health and software environment consistency. It is implemented as a Python library that hooks into the pre-initialization phase of distributed training frameworks like PyTorch or JAX. The architecture involves a distributed diagnostic engine that runs small, non-destructive test kernels across all allocated nodes. These tests verify NVLink and InfiniBand bandwidth, check for ECC errors on GPUs, and validate that all environment variables and dtypes match the job specification. It also queries a centralized metadata store to compare the current configuration against known 'good' job profiles. By identifying NCCL topology mismatches or library version conflicts before the main job starts, it prevents 'silent' performance degradation and immediate crashes that waste expensive compute time. The MVP would focus on a standalone CLI tool for Slurm-based clusters that outputs a 'Ready-to-Launch' health score and a detailed report of detected anomalies.

name: Firing Pin
tagline: Pre-flight validation system for GPU cluster jobs
description: A mission-critical validation tool for large-scale GPU clusters that runs comprehensive health and configuration checks before launching expensive training or inference jobs.
design_writeup:
Firing Pin is a mission-critical pre-flight validation system designed for large-scale GPU clusters. In high-performance computing environments where a single training run can cost thousands of dollars per hour, configuration errors (such as mismatched library versions or dead GPUs) are incredibly costly. Firing Pin acts as an automated 'check-list' that runs immediately before job execution, ensuring the environment is 100% ready for the workload.

The tool is implemented as a lightweight daemon and a set of integration plugins for common cluster managers like Slurm and Kubernetes. When a job is submitted, Firing Pin performs a series of parallelized checks: it pings all GPUs for health, validates driver versions, checks local disk space, and verifies weight accessibility. It uses a declarative configuration format where users can define custom 'health checks.' The tech stack would be primarily Go for performance and concurrency, with Python bindings for ease of use in ML scripts. A major challenge is performing these checks rapidly enough to not significantly delay job startup times. The MVP would focus on common failure modes on research clusters like Nautilus, providing a simple 'Pass/Fail' report and preventing the launch of any job that doesn't meet the specified criteria.
source_conversations: Medical device sales starting salary in Los Angeles

name: Firing Pin
tagline: Pre-flight validation for distributed GPU training
description: A validation framework to prevent training job failures by checking hardware configurations, resource availability, and NCCL versions before execution. It ensures cluster symmetry and prevents common setup errors from wasting compute time.
design_writeup: Firing Pin is a purpose-built validation framework designed to solve the "pre-flight" failure crisis in large-scale distributed GPU training environments. Training runs costing millions of dollars often fail within the first few minutes due to preventable configuration mismatches across thousands of nodes. Firing Pin operates as a validation wrapper around common orchestration and launcher tools like Slurm, Kubernetes, or torchrun. It performs a comprehensive "system health check" across all nodes in a cluster before the main training process is permitted to start. The architecture includes a lightweight agent written in Go that probes the local node's hardware state—checking GPU availability, peer-to-peer NVLink status, and IB (Infiniband) fabric health—alongside its software environment, verifying CUDA, PyTorch, and NCCL version parity. A central coordinator node then aggregates these reports to detect asymmetries, such as a single node using a mismatched NCCL version which would lead to a catastrophic silent deadlock later in the run. The technology stack leverages NVIDIA's NVML library for hardware telemetry and custom probes to test network interface throughput and latency between random node pairs. The biggest challenge is the heterogeneity of modern clusters; the tool must account for varied GPU architectures and interconnects while remaining agnostic to the specific training framework. name: Firing Pin Cluster Guardian
tagline: Autonomous health monitoring and validation for GPU clusters
description: A specialized infrastructure monitoring tool for large-scale ML training clusters. It detects silent data corruption, interconnect bottlenecks, and hardware degradation before they crash multi-million dollar training runs.
design_writeup:
Firing Pin is a distributed monitoring agent designed to manage the extreme unreliability of modern H100 and B200 GPU clusters. The system consists of a lightweight Rust-based daemon that runs on every node, communicating with a central orchestrator. Its primary function is 'Proactive Validation'—running continuous, non-intrusive micro-benchmarks to detect hardware failure modes that traditional metrics (like thermal or power usage) often miss. 

Technically, Firing Pin integrates deeply with the NVIDIA Management Library (NVML) and the NCCL collective communication library. It tracks GPU memory bit-flips, NVLink throughput, and IB (InfiniBand) retries in real-time. The architecture includes a 'pre-flight' check module that automatically validates the health of assigned nodes before a training job starts, ensuring that the ring topology of a distributed run is not bottlenecked by a single underperforming GPU. It features a high-resolution dashboard built with Grafana and a custom time-series backend optimized for high-cardinality telemetry data. 

The MVP scope involves a standalone CLI tool that can be integrated into Slurm or Kubernetes job scripts to perform a 60-second health check (NCCL-test and memory check) before launching a job. If any node fails, Firing Pin automatically triggers a node drain and alerts the infrastructure team. The main technical challenge is ensuring the monitoring daemon does not introduce jitter or performance overhead into the training process itself. The stack includes Rust for the node agent, Go for the orchestrator, and Prometheus/Grafana for the observability stack.

name: Franca
tagline: Formal verification for enterprise system migrations.
description: A tool that uses formal methods to mathematically prove that business logic in a new system perfectly matches the legacy system it replaces. It minimizes the risk of logical drift during high-stakes digital transformations.
design_writeup: Franca leverages the power of formal methods to bring mathematical certainty to the traditionally risky process of enterprise software migration. At its core is a specification-induction engine that analyzes legacy procedural code (VBA, COBOL, or PL/SQL) to generate a set of formal properties that the system must satisfy. These properties are then translated into a high-level specification language like TLA+. During the development of the replacement system, Franca continuously runs these specifications against the new code using an automated prover like Z3. This 'Continuous Verification' architecture ensures that any logic drift or calculation error is caught during the development phase rather than at go-live. The technical challenge is the 'state space explosion' when verifying complex financial workflows; Franca manages this by using modular verification techniques that break down the system into smaller, provable components. The MVP will focus on 'Balance Verification,' a tool that proves the migration of a ledger's summation logic is mathematically identical between two systems.

design_writeup: GPU Waste Watcher is an infrastructure monitoring and automated remediation tool for large-scale GPU clusters. It specifically targets the "idle resource" problem where researchers request high-end hardware (like A100s) but leave pods running with 0% utilization. The tool interfaces with Prometheus and Kubernetes APIs to track real-time GPU metrics. When a job is detected as idle for a set threshold (e.g., 45 minutes), the tool triggers a multi-stage remediation workflow: first, an automated warning to the user; second, a "graceful termination" period; and finally, a forced pod deletion to reclaim the hardware. The MVP includes a dashboard for admins to visualize "Value at Risk" (the dollar amount of wasted compute time) and a policy engine to set different idle thresholds based on GPU model and user priority. This project solves a massive operational failure in shared research environments by ensuring that expensive hardware is always available for active experiments.

It uses a specialized LLM-based ingestion engine to parse and normalize fragmented, non-digital permitting laws into a queryable format. The system integrates directly with construction management tools like Procore via a bi-directional API, ensuring that site-selection decisions are informed by current project timelines and regulatory risks. Technical challenges include the high frequency of data updates required for interconnection queues and the difficulty of acquiring non-public data from proprietary utility portals. The tech stack involves Python for data scraping and normalization, PostGIS for spatial data storage, and a React-based interactive map for the user interface. The MVP will focus on the most active datacenter markets, providing developers with a 'readiness score' for any given land parcel based on its proximity to high-voltage transmission lines and historical permitting speeds.

name: Hardware Codesign Framework
tagline: Optimizing software performance through gate-level architectural alignment.
description: A framework for developers to design software and hardware architectures in lockstep. It moves beyond standard abstraction layers to allow software requirements to dictate hardware logic gate configurations and vice-versa.
design_writeup:
The framework acts as an intermediate representation layer between high-level application code (C++/Rust) and Hardware Description Languages (Verilog/VHDL). It provides a library of pre-optimized hardware-software primitives that can be toggled based on the specific workload, such as cryptographic acceleration or specialized tensor operations. The core architecture uses an LLVM-based compiler backend that can output both binary executables and FPGA bitstreams.

Technical challenges include the immense complexity of cross-domain debugging and the long synthesis times required for hardware changes. The MVP would focus on a specific vertical, such as high-frequency trading or real-time signal processing, providing a set of 'golden' templates that show measurable performance gains over generic hardware. The software stack would rely on C++, LLVM, and specialized hardware simulation tools to allow developers to iterate on software without needing physical hardware in the early stages of design.

The MVP scope would focus on a spine-leaf data center fabric deployment, including a centralized management dashboard for firmware lifecycle management, automated configuration via Terraform, and basic telemetry visualization, effectively lowering the barrier to entry for large-scale software-defined networking.

The architecture integrates third-party APIs from utility providers and scrapes municipal records to track zoning changes and environmental review statuses in real-time. This allows site selection teams to bypass the traditional 12-18 month due diligence phase. The primary challenge is the data acquisition of non-public utility grid capacities; the platform addresses this by creating a secure portal for electrical cooperatives to list 'excess capacity nodes' anonymously to attract developers. The MVP will focus on high-growth industrial corridors, mapping existing substations and fiber backbones to provide a definitive directory of sites ready for immediate data center construction.

name: JaxMobile
tagline: A native JAX runtime and HAL for mobile NPU acceleration
description: An optimized mobile runtime that allows JAX models to run directly on Android and iOS hardware without complex conversion to TensorFlow Lite, solving the edge-inference gap for JAX research.
design_writeup: JaxMobile is a runtime environment designed to bring the power of JAX research to mobile edge devices. The core functionality is to provide a direct execution path from JAX primitives to mobile hardware accelerators (NPUs and GPUs) by implementing a specialized Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL). The architecture avoids the traditional JAX-to-TFLite conversion path, instead using MLIR to lower JAX models into device-specific instructions. The tech stack involves C++ for the runtime core and MLIR for the compilation pipeline. A major technical challenge is implementing a memory-efficient JIT compiler for mobile environments that can handle JAX's functional transformation logic. The MVP scope includes support for standard Transformer kernels on Android devices using Qualcomm or MediaTek NPUs. This project addresses the coordination problem in the Android ecosystem by providing a high-performance, JAX-native alternative to TFLite, enabling researchers to deploy frontier architectures directly to mobile.

name: Jukebox
tagline: Hostilely opinionated infrastructure for sane startups.
description: An infrastructure-as-a-service platform that enforces a single, battle-tested stack. It prevents engineering bikeshedding by making custom configurations difficult and defaults mandatory.
design_writeup: Jukebox is a 'hostile onboarding' platform designed for consumer and enterprise SaaS startups that do not need—and should not have—custom infrastructure. The platform enforces a strict, hyper-optimized stack: Next.js, Postgres, and Redis running on AWS. There is zero flexibility; the system handles scaling, CI/CD, and backups automatically using an 'opinionated-as-fuck' configuration. If a developer wants to add a custom message queue or change a database setting, the platform essentially tells them 'no.' For those who insist, Jukebox provides a 'gun to shoot yourself with' feature: it will eject the raw Terraform and config files to the user, at which point the automated support and managed reliability are revoked. This architecture eliminates the need for early-stage DevOps hires and prevents the 'category 1' startups from wasting months on AWS architecture. The technical stack involves a custom control plane built on top of AWS CDK that manages multi-tenant isolation and automated yielding for Postgres instances. The MVP is a CLI tool that takes a Next.js repo and deploys the full Jukebox-managed environment in under 60 seconds.

name: Krizhevsky NCCL
tagline: Specialized CUDA library for sparse cross-GPU communication
description: A CUDA/NCCL sub-library designed for selective model parallelism, inspired by the original AlexNet architecture's two-GPU split. It allows for high-performance training where inter-GPU communication is intentionally limited to specific layers, reducing NVLink congestion.
design_writeup: Krizhevsky NCCL is a research-oriented communication library that revisits the 'cope' of the 2012 era as a modern architectural choice for extreme-scale training. The core functionality allows developers to define a 'sparse synchronization topology' where gradient updates are not shared globally (All-Reduce) for every layer. Instead, it provides primitives for gated synchronization, enabling a model to fit across multiple GPUs while only communicating activations or gradients at designated 'bottleneck' layers. The technical stack includes C++, CUDA, and deep integration with NCCL collectives. The library's main challenge is maintaining training stability and convergence when gradients are partially decoupled across the cluster. The MVP scope involves a set of modified collective operations that can be integrated into PyTorch as custom autograd functions, along with a profiling tool to measure the speedup in training throughput versus the impact on loss convergence. This is particularly useful for hardware configurations with asymmetric interconnect bandwidths where global synchronization is the primary bottleneck.
source_conversations: Krizhevsky CUDA library naming idea and experiment in gilpins lab
claude_prompt:
Build Krizhevsky NCCL: A CUDA/NCCL sub-library designed for selective model parallelism, inspired by the original AlexNet architecture's two-GPU split. It allows for high-performance training where inter-GPU communication is intentionally limited to specific layers, reducing NVLink congestion.

name: Legacy Code Skeleton Key
tagline: Formal verification and transpilation for VBA/PLSQL to Postgres
description: A deep-tech transpilation engine that uses formal methods to move legacy business logic from VBA and PL/SQL into modern, scalable Postgres environments. It provides a 'skeleton key' to break vendor lock-in for enterprises trapped by legacy code.
design_writeup:
The Legacy Code Skeleton Key is a program synthesis and verification platform designed to handle the 'impossible' task of migrating legacy enterprise logic. Unlike simple regex-based transpilers, this engine utilizes neurosymbolic methods and formal logic to ensure semantic equivalence between the source (e.g., a complex Excel VBA macro or an Oracle PL/SQL stored procedure) and the target (Postgres-compatible SQL or Python). 

The architecture involves three stages: 1) Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) extraction and normalization, 2) Formal Modeling, where the business logic is converted into a mathematical representation of its state changes, and 3) Synthesis, where the engine generates high-performance, idiomatic Postgres code. A core component is the 'Equivalence Checker,' which runs side-by-side execution traces of the legacy and modern code with thousands of edge-case inputs to prove they behave identically. 

The MVP will focus specifically on migrating Oracle PL/SQL stored procedures to Postgres. The tech stack uses OCaml for the formal methods engine, Python for the high-level orchestration, and a custom Dockerized environment for side-by-side validation testing. The main challenge is handling non-deterministic or environment-dependent behaviors in legacy VBA, requiring a robust emulation layer during the verification process. This product aims to liberate the $170B+ trapped in legacy license revenue by providing a guaranteed path off the proprietary monoliths.
source_conversations: Website design and first impressions for AI businesses
claude_prompt:

name: Llamaform
tagline: Declarative infrastructure and policy enforcement for LLM apps
description: A 'Terraform for LLMs' that provides runtime constraints and security policy enforcement, ensuring language models stay within operational bounds in production environments.
design_writeup: Llamaform is an infrastructure-as-code tool for AI applications, focusing on the deployment, versioning, and safety of LLM workflows. The core functionality allows developers to define LLM behavior using declarative configuration files, which are then enforced by a runtime proxy. The architecture consists of a high-performance gateway that intercepts model calls and applies safety filters, cost limits, and deterministic constraints. The tech stack includes Go for the high-concurrency proxy and Python for the configuration parser. A primary technical challenge is implementing low-latency policy enforcement that doesn't significantly degrade the user experience of real-time chat. The MVP scope includes a YAML-based policy engine that can block specific topics, limit token usage per user, and version-control prompts. This addresses the critical enterprise pain point of delegating tasks to non-deterministic systems by providing a programmable safety layer that prevents models from doing 'catastrophically stupid' things in production.

name: MD-TPU Accelerator
tagline: Specialized silicon for real-time molecular dynamics simulation
description: A custom ASIC designed to accelerate the complex force calculations required for high-fidelity molecular dynamics simulations. It aims to reduce drug discovery timelines by allowing researchers to simulate protein interactions at millisecond timescales that are currently impossible on generic GPUs.
design_writeup: The MD-TPU Accelerator is a domain-specific architecture (DSA) optimized for the high-performance computing requirements of molecular dynamics (MD). Unlike general-purpose GPUs, which are limited by memory bandwidth when calculating irregular atom interactions, the MD-TPU utilizes a specialized systolic array of processing elements (PEs) designed specifically for Lennard-Jones and Coulombic force evaluations. The architecture includes a custom low-latency interconnect that allows for rapid updates of neighbor lists, which are typically a major bottleneck in MD simulations. On the software side, the project includes a compiler that maps common MD frameworks like GROMACS and OpenMM directly onto the chip's instruction set, bypassing the overhead of traditional driver stacks. The system is designed to scale horizontally across thousands of nodes in a data center environment, enabling millisecond-scale simulations of large protein complexes. Key technical challenges include designing a hardware-based grid-cell hashing mechanism for fast spatial sorting and ensuring high-precision floating-point arithmetic within tight power constraints. The MVP involves the development of the ISA (Instruction Set Architecture), a bit-accurate software simulator, and an initial FPGA prototype capable of simulating 100,000-atom systems.

name: Mainframe Strangler Hypervisor
tagline: Risk-free mainframe-to-cloud migration through emulation
description: A compatibility hypervisor that runs legacy COBOL and z-series transactions in a modern cloud environment, allowing companies to migrate workloads one-by-one without altering core business logic.

name: MoE Cache Optimizer
tagline: Predictive expert prefetching for Mixture of Experts models
description: A system-level optimization tool that predicts which 'experts' will be activated in an MoE model before they are needed. It proactively warms the cache to reduce VRAM requirements and inference latency.
design_writeup: The core functionality centers on 'sparsity prediction.' By analyzing early-layer routing signals in MoE architectures (like Mixtral or DeepSeek), the system identifies the most likely FFN layers to be triggered. It then manages a proactive memory hierarchy, fetching weights from system RAM to GPU VRAM ahead of execution. This requires custom CUDA kernels that can handle asynchronous weight loading without blocking the main computation stream. The MVP would be a C++ library that integrates with vLLM or Hugging Face to optimize memory utilization for 100B+ parameter models on limited hardware.

name: NeoLinear Attention
tagline: Scaling context beyond 5 million tokens using PINNs.
description: A research project focusing on scaling LLM context windows by combining Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) with Linear Attention mechanisms.
design_writeup: NeoLinear Attention is a research-heavy project that aims to break the O(n²) complexity barrier of standard Transformers. The project explores the intersection of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and Linear Attention mechanisms to enable context windows exceeding 5 million tokens. The core architecture treats the attention mechanism as a continuous dynamical system governed by ordinary differential equations (ODEs). By applying PINN techniques, the model learns to approximate these state transitions more efficiently than discrete recurrence. This allows memory requirements to stay constant with respect to sequence length and computation to remain linear. The technical stack is built on JAX for high-performance numerical computing and custom CUDA kernels for specific linear attention scan operations. The framework includes a Beyond-Attention library that provides drop-in replacements for standard attention layers in existing architectures. The primary challenge is maintaining numerical stability and expressive power when compressing long-term dependencies into a finite state vector. The MVP scope includes a 1B-parameter model implementation that can successfully perform needle-in-a-haystack retrieval tasks across context lengths of 1 million tokens, demonstrating significantly lower VRAM usage compared to standard Transformers.

It takes inputs like total GPU count, thermal limits, and power availability to generate a complete Revit building information model (BIM). The software uses a constraint-satisfaction engine to calculate optimal cable lengths for NVLink/InfiniBand fabrics, ensuring that the latency between nodes is uniform—a requirement for turning NCCL variables into constants and squeezing out maximum performance.

A major challenge is the physical variability of existing data centers; the tool must allow for deviations while highlighting the 'performance cost' of such changes. The MVP scope would include a web-based configurator for GB200 NVL72 racks and the ability to export standardized wiring diagrams and cooling clearance reports. The tech stack would utilize Python for the logic engine and a Three.js-based web viewer for 3D layout visualization.

name: OOM Predictor for ML
tagline: Static memory analysis to prevent Out-Of-Memory errors in training jobs.
description: A static analysis tool for ML compute graphs that calculates memory requirements to predict Out-Of-Memory (OOM) failures before jobs are submitted. It helps researchers optimize resource requests on shared clusters.
design_writeup: OOM-Predictor is a pre-submission utility that traces the compute graph of a machine learning model to calculate its exact peak memory footprint. It accounts for model weights based on parameter counts, activation memory based on tensor shapes, and additional gradient memory required for backpropagation. The tool allows researchers to specify their hardware and provides a pass/fail prediction for their proposed batch size and sequence length. By using torch.fx for graph tracing, the tool can accurately model intermediate tensor lifetimes and memory reuse strategies. This prevents the submit-and-wait cycle where jobs fail several hours into training. The MVP is a CLI tool and a GitHub Action that researchers can use to validate their training scripts against their target hardware specifications.
source_conversations: The actual dev tools that I want to build for submitting jobs to nautilus

name: OOM-Leak Diagnostic Utility
tagline: Automated log aggregation and bug reporting for Linux memory failures
description: A CLI tool that automatically captures system state, kernel logs, and process tree snapshots during Out-Of-Memory (OOM) events. It provides a standardized report to help Linux users and developers identify massive memory leaks in system utilities like apt or gnome-shell.
design_writeup: The core functionality is a system daemon that listens for OOM-killer signals via the systemd-oomd socket or monitors /var/log/syslog for kernel OOM kills. Upon detection, it automatically triggers a data collection routine that pulls the last 500 lines of dmesg, current journalctl logs, and a snapshot of the process list (RSS vs. VZ usage). The tech stack would be built in Rust for low memory footprint and high performance, as it needs to run reliably during system thrashing. It would interface with the Linux kernel's /proc filesystem to calculate precise anonymous memory usage. The challenge is ensuring the tool itself doesn't exacerbate memory pressure during a leak. The MVP scope involves a CLI tool that can be run after a crash to 'autopsy' the failure, generating a pre-formatted bug report for platforms like Launchpad or GitHub, including specific details like UID, anon-rss, and pgtables entries found in the kernel's killed process message.
source_conversations: Should make an automated library for reporting bugs to Ubuntu that give all these logs from crashed processes

The architectural focus is on modularity, allowing a customer like Microsoft to add their own custom logic for Azure-specific offloads directly onto the licensed silicon design. The software component includes an open-source driver layer compatible with SONiC (Software for Open Networking in the Cloud). This solves the vertical integration bottleneck for large clouds by lowering the R&D barrier to entry for custom networking silicon.

design_writeup: OpenHCI is a web-based control plane for managing clusters of Linux servers running KVM and Ceph (for distributed storage). It provides a high-level abstraction for 'Hyperconverged Infrastructure,' allowing admins to provision VMs, manage virtual networks (SDN), and monitor cluster health without using the CLI. The architecture follows a microservices model: a 'Manager' node provides the API and UI, while 'Agent' nodes run on every hypervisor to execute commands via libvirt. The UI would be a modern SPA (Single Page Application) using React and a real-time websocket connection for telemetry. The MVP scope would include VM lifecycle management (create, start, stop, migrate), basic virtual networking (VLANs), and a dashboard for Ceph storage status. The main challenge is handling high-availability (HA) failovers gracefully and ensuring compatibility across various Linux distributions.

name: PlateTrace
tagline: Automated parking duration tracking via License Plate Recognition.
description: A monitoring solution for parking lot operators that uses LPR cameras to automatically track how long vehicles remain in a lot. It eliminates manual enforcement by matching entry and exit events to identify overstays.
design_writeup: PlateTrace is a software platform designed to automate the monitoring and management of parking lot utilization through License Plate Recognition (LPR) technology. The primary goal is to provide lot operators with a defensible, automated way to track vehicle dwell time, replacing manual patrols and physical ticketing systems. The core functionality includes real-time entry and exit logging, duration calculation, and automated alerting for overstays. The system architecture follows an edge-to-cloud model. High-resolution cameras installed at entry and exit points perform initial image capture. These images are sent to a processing engine—either on-site or in the cloud—that uses Computer Vision and Optical Character Recognition (OCR) to extract license plate text and jurisdiction. The processed data is stored in a time-series database like TimescaleDB to facilitate fast queries on vehicle residency times. The backend, built with Python (FastAPI), manages the business logic for calculating stay durations and matching entrance/exit events based on plate identifiers. Key technical challenges include handling plate 'noise' (e.g., mud, specialized fonts, or low-light conditions) and reconciling 'orphaned' entries where a vehicle's exit was missed. To mitigate these, the system will implement fuzzy matching algorithms and temporal logic to filter out impossible stay durations. The MVP scope will include a management dashboard for viewing current lot occupancy, historical reports on average stay times, and a notification system that alerts operators when a specific plate has exceeded its authorized duration. This provides a clear path toward automated enforcement and data-driven lot management.

name: PlesioSync Compiler
tagline: Static scheduling for dataflow-informed interconnects
description: A compiler-hardware co-design framework that treats the network interconnect as a programmable primitive. It allows developers to statically schedule data movement across multi-chip AI architectures to eliminate runtime overhead.
design_writeup: The PlesioSync Compiler is designed for architectures like Groq's LPX or Tenstorrent's Tensix cores, where memory and compute are tightly coupled and statically predictable. Instead of relying on a dynamic runtime to manage packet switching and network traffic, this compiler maps the computational graph of a model directly to a temporal schedule of data transfers. The architecture treats chip-to-chip communication as part of the program itself, allowing for cycle-accurate synchronization across thousands of cores. The primary challenge is managing clock drift in plesiosynchronous systems and optimizing for the massive die area constraints of SRAM-only hardware. The MVP would include a static scheduler for a 4-chip cluster running a Transformer-based inference pass.

name: Portable Mojo Backend
tagline: Researcher-friendly ML portability across diverse hardware.
description: A custom compiler backend for the Mojo programming language that allows researchers to run high-performance kernels on non-NVIDIA hardware like Intel XPUs. It brings researcher-friendly syntax to the world of hardware acceleration.
design_writeup: This project aims to solve the portability gap in AI hardware by building a Mojo backend that targets diverse accelerators beyond NVIDIA GPUs. The technical design leverages the MLIR (Multi-Level Intermediate Representation) framework to bridge Mojo's high-level syntax with hardware-specific execution models like Intel's Gaudi or xPUs. The developer writes custom dialects and lowerings that map Mojo's tiling and parallelization abstractions to the specific memory hierarchies and compute primitives of the target silicon. This approach is particularly valuable for researchers working on platforms like the National Research Platform (Nautilus), which often feature a heterogeneous mix of hardware. The backend allows for '80% of the power' of custom CUDA kernels but is accessible to scientists who prefer Python-like syntax. The MVP scope includes a Docker-ready environment where a researcher can compile a standard ML model (like a Neural ODE) and have it execute efficiently on whatever hardware is available, effectively turning a 'scrap yard' of diverse GPUs into a unified compute pool.
source_conversations: Custom AI chip race heats up

name: Postgres-Enterprise-X
tagline: An Oracle-grade database engine built on a scalable PostgreSQL core.
description: A high-performance database solution designed to compete with Oracle by extending PostgreSQL with horizontal scaling, autonomous machine-learning-driven tuning, and an Oracle-to-Postgres compatibility layer for enterprise legacy applications.
design_writeup:
Postgres-Enterprise-X is a specialized fork of PostgreSQL optimized for large-scale enterprise environments. Its core functionality is built on three pillars: distributed horizontal scaling, autonomous tuning, and high-fidelity compatibility. Unlike standard PostgreSQL, this engine utilizes a 'coordinator-worker' architecture that shards data across a cluster of nodes, allowing it to handle petabyte-scale datasets that are traditionally the domain of Oracle or Teradata.

The technical architecture leverages C for the core engine modifications to improve the query optimizer for complex joins and Go for the orchestration layer that manages the cluster. A unique feature is the 'AutoTuner' sidecar—a reinforcement learning agent that monitors real-time metrics such as buffer cache hit ratios and lock contention to dynamically adjust kernel parameters and suggest index creations. To facilitate migration, the database includes a PL/SQL translation layer that allows most Oracle stored procedures to run natively on the Postgres core with minimal modification.

Significant technical challenges include maintaining strict ACID compliance across distributed nodes and matching the performance of Oracle's proprietary RAC (Real Application Clusters). The MVP will focus on a 'Read-Replica Scaling' model with a single write-master, an automated indexing advisor, and a basic PL/SQL-to-PL/pgSQL conversion tool. This approach targets organizations looking to reduce massive licensing costs while maintaining the reliability and feature set they expect from top-tier enterprise databases.

name: Project DIME
tagline: A high-level abstraction layer for NVIDIA's NCCL primitives.
description: Distributed Interconnect Management Environment (DIME) simplifies the hair-pulling experience of getting multiple GPUs to talk to each other. It provides a clean abstraction over raw NCCL calls, managing topology discovery, communicator layout, and algorithm preferences automatically.
design_writeup: Project DIME is designed to bridge the gap between low-level communication primitives (NCCL) and high-level training frameworks (PyTorch/Jax). The core challenge in distributed training is that NCCL expects the user to know their exact hardware topology (NVLink vs. PCIe) and algorithm preferences. DIME solves this by implementing a 'Topology Auto-Discovery' module that runs at initialization to map the interconnect graph. Based on this graph, it dynamically selects the optimal collective algorithm (e.g., ring vs. tree) for specific message sizes. Architecturally, it is a C++ library with Python bindings that wraps the `ncclCommInitRank` and collective operations. It automates the 'rendezvous' process, managing the distributed store and process group creation so researchers don't have to deal with raw socket configurations. The MVP will include a Python API that replaces the standard `torch.distributed.init_process_group` with a single `dime.init()` call, which handles all environmental setup and provides performance-optimized defaults for standard operations like all-reduce and all-gather on multi-node clusters.
claude_prompt:

It provides a clean abstraction over raw NCCL calls, managing topology discovery, communicator layout, and algorithm preferences automatically.

name: Rete-Attention Engine
tagline: Scaling context windows using Rete-based pattern matching
description: An experimental transformer architecture that integrates the Rete Algorithm's matching efficiency into attention mechanisms. It aims to solve the quadratic scaling problem of context windows by only updating 'changed' attention patterns.
design_writeup: The design explores the parallel between Rete's incremental matching (avoiding recomputation of unchanged rule conditions) and the attention mechanism's need to process long sequences. It involves building a network of 'partial match' nodes that persist across window updates, reducing the O(n^2) cost to something closer to linear for incremental context. Technical challenges include implementing this on modern GPU hardware that favors dense matrix operations over sparse networks. The MVP would be a small-scale proof-of-concept model (125M params) that maintains a larger-than-standard context with low latency.

name: Rivendell
tagline: The open-source alternative to Palantir Foundry and Snowflake for enterprise data.
description: Rivendell is an open-source data integration and operational platform designed to replicate the core capabilities of Palantir Foundry. It provides a unified environment for data engineering, analytics, and application building by abstracting complex data into a business-ready ontology.
design_writeup:
Rivendell is designed as a Kubernetes-native microservices platform that unifies the data lifecycle from raw storage to operational decision-making. The core functionality centers on an 'Ontology Layer'—a semantic mapping system that translates technical database tables into real-world objects and relationships, allowing non-technical users to interact with data. 

The recommended tech stack prioritizes high-scale, vendor-neutral components. The Infrastructure Layer uses Kubernetes for orchestration with Istio or Linkerd for a secure service mesh. For the Storage Layer, Apache Iceberg is used as the table format to provide ACID transactions and schema evolution over S3-compatible blob storage, ensuring compatibility with multiple engines. The Compute Layer is engine-agnostic, leveraging Apache Spark for batch processing, Apache Flink for real-time streaming, and Ray for distributed machine learning tasks. This allows the platform to handle diverse workloads without being locked into a single processing paradigm.

Key architecture decisions include a zero-trust security model. Identity management is handled via Keycloak, while fine-grained, attribute-based access control (ABAC) is enforced through Open Policy Agent (OPA) at every tier of the stack. This ensures that data governance is baked into the platform rather than being an afterthought. 

The main technical challenges lie in the 'Data Lineage' and 'Integration' components—reclaiming the seamless 'it just works' experience of proprietary clones while maintaining open-source modularity. The MVP scope focuses on the core ontology management UI, Iceberg integration, and Spark-based data transformations. Future features will involve high-level visual application builders and automated data health monitoring. Rivendell is viable because it addresses the massive enterprise demand for Palantir-like capabilities without the high cost and vendor lock-in associated with proprietary data platforms.

tagline: The TSMC for the emerging robotics revolution
description: A specialized contract manufacturing platform that acts as an OEM for software companies building robotics. It handles the complex mechanical hardware production, allowing AI companies to focus on software while maintaining a 'Made in USA' hardware stack.
design_writeup: The core functionality of RoboFoundry is to provide a standardized hardware abstraction layer (HAL) for robotic components, allowing software companies to 'print' physical robot iterations similar to how chip designers use TSMC. name: SafeGate OS
tagline: A hardened Linux distribution designed specifically to stop elderly tech scams.
description: A custom Linux-based operating system for older hardware that hard-disables remote access and uses AI to filter phishing and scam-related web traffic.
design_writeup: SafeGate OS is built on a lightweight Debian base, optimized for the older laptops and desktops common among senior citizens. Its primary innovation is a 'Scam-Proof Kernel' where common remote desktop protocols (RDP, VNC, and third-party tools like TeamViewer) are stripped from the OS entirely, making it impossible for a scammer to 'remote in' even if the user is convinced to try. The user interface is highly simplified, featuring large icons and a locked-down browser environment. The browser includes a pre-configured DNS filter that blocks known scam domains and uses an AI-inference engine to detect phishing patterns in real-time. If a user visits a site that triggers an 'Urgent Security Alert' (a common scam tactic), the OS intercepts the window and displays a full-screen, un-closable warning that provides the phone number of a trusted family member. The architecture utilizes a 'Read-Only' system partition to prevent malware persistence, ensuring that every reboot returns the system to a clean, safe state. For updates, it uses a background 'A/B' atomic update system to ensure the user never has to interact with terminal windows or complex installation prompts.

The developer utilizes hardware-specific features like unified memory architecture to avoid costly data copies between the CPU and GPU. The project scope includes benchmarking the driver against standard Vulkan and OpenCL implementations using complex ML models like Transformers and Neural ODEs to demonstrate superior FLOP utilization on the edge.

The project aims to provide aggressive static checking of tensor operations, tracking dimensions, device location (CPU vs GPU), and numerical precision at the compilation level. Unlike existing frameworks that fail at runtime with cryptic CUDA errors, Steel Toe ensures that if a model compiles, it is mathematically consistent and resource-efficient. For cluster administrators, Steel Toe serves as an access control layer: junior researchers or students must pass Steel Toe's rigorous checks to gain access to premium compute resources like A100/H100 clusters. The technical design leverages Mojo's ability to interface directly with MLIR and custom kernels, allowing for deep introspection of the computation graph before a single byte of memory is allocated on a GPU.

name: Synaptics Custom Linux Driver
tagline: Custom touchpad precision and gestures for Linux laptops.
description: A custom input driver for Synaptics touchpads on Linux, specifically targeting Dell Inspiron hardware. It is for Linux power users who find generic drivers lacking in sensitivity or gesture support, solving the problem of poor hardware-specific input performance.
design_writeup: The driver is designed as a modular input module for the Linux kernel, specifically targeting the Synaptics RMI4 or SMBus protocols commonly found in Dell Inspiron hardware. Its core functionality is to bridge the gap between low-level hardware interrupts and the high-level input events expected by X11 or Wayland compositors. The architecture follows the standard Linux Input Subsystem model, where the driver reads raw byte streams from the I2C or PS/2 bus, decodes the proprietary packet format, and emits input events via the evdev interface. The tech stack primarily involves C for the kernel-level code, utilizing the input_report_abs and input_sync functions to transmit coordinate data. A significant portion of the logic will be dedicated to palm rejection and noise filtering, which are common pain points with generic drivers. This involves implementing a spatial-temporal filter to distinguish between intentional finger movement and accidental contact. For multi-touch gestures, the driver will integrate with libinput, providing the necessary metadata to support pinch-to-zoom and three-finger swipes. The project will also include a user-space daemon that interacts with the driver via /sys or /proc files, allowing users to customize sensitivity curves and gesture mappings without recompiling the kernel. The main technical challenge lies in reverse-engineering specific Synaptics features that may not be well-documented in the public ACPI tables. The MVP scope will include reliable cursor tracking, primary and secondary click detection, and basic two-finger scrolling.

name: Tails Network Observability
tagline: Cloudvision-style telemetry for open-source SONiC infrastructure.
description: An abstraction and observability layer built on top of SONiC-based networking hardware. It provides high-level telemetry and APIs similar to hyperscale internal systems for large-scale cluster management.
design_writeup: Tails is designed to provide enterprise-grade network visibility on top of commoditized white-box hardware running SONiC (Software for Open Networking in the Cloud). The core functionality is a distributed telemetry agent written in Go that sits on each individual switch, collecting real-time data directly from the ASIC via the SONiC Redis database and the Switch Abstraction Interface (SAI). This data is then streamed to a centralized controller cluster using gRPC for high-throughput, low-latency communication. The tech stack would leverage a time-series database like VictoriaMetrics or ClickHouse for long-term storage and high-speed querying of millions of data points per second. The architecture follows a classic spine-leaf observability pattern, where the Tails controller aggregates data to provide a single pane of glass view of traffic patterns, micro-bursts, packet drop counts, and per-hop latency across the entire fabric. A major technical challenge is handling the immense volume of telemetry data in 400G and 800G environments without taxing the switch CPU, requiring efficient on-box filtering, sampling, and compression algorithms. The MVP would focus on providing a Vision API for network health, allowing infrastructure engineers to query the real-time state of the cluster using natural language or structured JSON, effectively bypassing the need for manual CLI scraping or fragmented vendor-specific management tools used by traditional networking companies.

name: Thermal Bridge Optimizer
tagline: Integrating chip-level heat maps into datacenter cooling models
description: A software bridge that imports fine-grained thermal data from silicon design tools (Synopsys/Cadence) into datacenter CFD models. It enables hyperscalers to optimize rack-level cooling for high-density AI clusters.
design_writeup: The Thermal Bridge Optimizer breaks the abstraction barrier between chip design and datacenter operations. It takes power density maps and switching activity data from place-and-route tools and translates them into localized heat-source inputs for Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations of the rack and hall. This allows for the design of liquid cooling systems and air-flow baffles that are specifically tuned to the 'hotspots' on individual GPUs (like H100 or Blackwell chips). The architecture involves a data translation layer that converts SPICE or RTL power models into boundary conditions for solvers like OpenFOAM. The tech stack would use Python for data processing and C++ for the high-performance CFD integration. The challenge is the massive difference in spatial scales (micrometers on-chip to meters in the datacenter). The MVP would be a plugin for existing datacenter management software that allows operators to import a 'thermal digital twin' of a specific GPU rack to simulate cooling efficiency under different workload intensities.

name: Topology-Aware NCCL Tuner
tagline: Optimizing GPU collectives for die-to-die interconnect performance.
description: A performance optimization tool for massive GPU clusters that identifies on-package die relationships to tune NCCL communication. It aims to recover 10-20% of performance currently lost to topology-blind collective operations.
design_writeup: High-performance training clusters using next-generation GPU packages (like Nvidia Rubin or Blackwell) suffer from a 'topology blindness' where the NVIDIA Collective Communications Library (NCCL) treats all GPUs in a rack as having equivalent hop costs. This tool implements a hardware-level discovery layer that queries the specific die-to-die (D2D) vs package-to-package links within a node. By identifying that two logical GPUs are actually dies residing on the same package, the tuner can inject custom communication graphs into NCCL that prioritize intra-package fabric (NVLink C2C) over external NVLink switches. The architecture consists of a kernel-level driver to read hardware affinity and a user-space library that hooks into the NCCL API. The main challenge is handling the bandwidth asymmetry at scale, where misconfigured collectives lead to congestion on the top-of-rack switches. The MVP will provide a benchmarking suite that generates optimal NCCL_TOPO_FILE configurations for specific hyperscaler instances (e.g., AWS P5/P6), specifically targeting large-scale All-Reduce operations used in LLM training.

name: TrustCall Protocol
tagline: Cryptographic verification for the global telephone network.
description: A digital signature framework for telecommunications that uses a certificate chain of trust to verify caller identity. It prevents caller ID spoofing by requiring carriers to sign calls at the source, allowing receiving networks to validate the legitimacy of every incoming call in real-time.
design_writeup:
The TrustCall Protocol addresses the systemic vulnerability of the global telephone network: the lack of caller ID authentication. Currently, the 'From' field in a SIP header is easily spoofed by bad actors. This project proposes a hierarchical Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) specifically optimized for the low-latency requirements of global telephony. 

The architecture consists of three tiers: Root Authorities (international bodies or national regulators like the FCC), Intermediate CAs (national telecom agencies), and End-Entity Certificates (licensed carriers). Each carrier receives a certificate authorizing them to originate calls from specific number ranges. When a call is initiated, the originating carrier generates a digital signature using their private key. This signature covers the caller ID, a timestamp to prevent replay attacks, and a hash of the routing path. This data is embedded into extended SIP headers.

Technical Stack: The core verification engine should be built in Rust or C++ for maximum throughput, as signature verification must happen in milliseconds during the call setup phase. Verification results should be cached in a distributed Redis layer to avoid redundant cryptographic operations for high-volume routes. The system would utilize Elliptic Curve Cryptography (Ed25519) for its small signature size and high performance compared to RSA.

The primary technical challenge is the 'International Gateway Problem.' Calls arriving from non-participating countries will lack signatures. The design handles this through a 'Tiered Trust' model: calls are labeled as 'Verified,' 'Unverified,' or 'Potential Spam' on the user's handset based on the presence and validity of the signature. 

An MVP would involve an implementation of the verification logic within an open-source PBX like Asterisk, demonstrating cross-carrier validation within a private sandbox. Future features would include a global Certificate Revocation List (CRL) for carriers found to be harboring spammers, effectively blacklisting entire network segments at the protocol level. This is unique because it moves the solution from 'detection' (which is reactive) to 'authentication' (which is proactive).
claude_prompt:
Build TrustCall Protocol: A digital signature framework for telecommunications that uses a certificate chain of trust to verify caller identity. It prevents caller ID spoofing by requiring carriers to sign calls at the source, allowing receiving networks to validate the legitimacy of every incoming call in real-time.

design_writeup: Turtles utilizes a hierarchical scheduling model where every node in the cluster can act as a local scheduler for its sub-resources. The tech stack would likely involve Go or C++ for performance, utilizing an actor-model for managing task state and recursion. Key challenges include avoiding infinite loops in recursive task spawning and ensuring fair resource allocation across deep hierarchies. The MVP would focus on basic recursive task submission and resource isolation.

description: A unified driver framework and gesture engine for Linux touchpads that brings macOS-level palm rejection and fluid gestures to various distributions. It uses machine learning to improve touch accuracy and hardware compatibility.
design_writeup: UniTouch Linux is an ambitious infrastructure project aimed at solving the fragmented and often subpar touchpad experience on Linux distributions. The project will develop a unified, high-level driver framework that sits between the kernel and the desktop environment. The tech stack involves a core engine written in C for low-level performance, leveraging the existing libinput library as a foundation while adding a sophisticated gesture-processing layer. It also includes a 'Gesture Synthesis Engine' that allows users to map complex multi-touch movements to system actions via a D-Bus API. A major challenge is the diversity of hardware protocols, such as I2C and HID over I2C, which requires the driver to be highly adaptable to various controller signatures. The project will also face the challenge of achieving 'zero-perceived-latency' for gesture tracking. The MVP scope will include a standalone daemon for X11 and Wayland that provides advanced palm rejection and a set of customizable three-finger gestures, along with a simple GTK-based configuration utility. By standardizing the touchpad interaction model, UniTouch Linux aims to make the Linux desktop a more viable alternative for professional developers.

The platform acts as a series of specialized adapters that wrap around legacy databases (SQL Server, Oracle) or non-standard APIs (SOAP, XML). The core architecture is a 'Unified Data Model' (UDM) that maps disparate data fields from different software into a single, standardized JSON schema.
